death.cause: killed in action at Port Hudson, Louisiana

military_service.notes: Killed Jul 22, 1864 at Port Hudson, Louisiana.

bio.photo_notes: Civil War tintype, c. 1860-1869. Per Sam's attribution decision: this is HD0951 PVT Levi H. Bartlett (Co. B, 35th Wisconsin Infantry); the Wisconsin Historical Society catalog (which lists it as 'Co. G, 37th Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry') is presumed to have a unit transcription error since the image is held in a Wisconsin source for a Wisconsin soldier.

burial.plot_notes: Chalmette National Cemetery, Chalmette, St. Bernard Parish LA (per Findagrave).

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in action Port Hudson, Louisiana, July 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Levi H. Bartlett was killed on July 22, 1864 at the vicinity of Port Hudson Louisiana during the Civil War, at about age forty-two. He was serving as a private in Company B, Thirty-fifth Wisconsin Infantry — having enlisted from Wisconsin despite his Vermont origin. The Port Hudson siege was a critical Union operation to control the lower Mississippi River; the fortified Confederate position had finally fallen the year before, but skirmishes and casualties continued throughout 1864.

narrative_blocks.header: Vermont birth, Wisconsin enlistment, Louisiana death
narrative_blocks.body: Levi H. Bartlett was born around 1822 in Vermont. By the time of his Civil War enlistment he was living in Wisconsin and joined the Thirty-fifth Wisconsin Infantry — the cross-country movement from his New England birthplace to his midwestern enlistment was characteristic of mid-nineteenth-century American settler migration. His death in Louisiana completed a Vermont-Wisconsin-Louisiana arc spanning his lifetime.

narrative_blocks.header: Left widow Elizabeth; buried Chalmette National
narrative_blocks.body: Levi was survived by his widow Elizabeth Bartlett. He is buried at Chalmette National Cemetery in Saint Bernard Parish Louisiana — the federally-administered military cemetery for Union soldiers killed in the lower Mississippi River campaigns of the Civil War. The cemetery clusters thousands of Union dead from operations at Port Hudson, New Orleans, and surrounding theaters of the western Confederacy collapse.

burial.plot_notes: Brick Church Cemetery, Greenbush, Sheboygan County WI, Row 7 (per Findagrave).

military_service.unit: Capt Robbins's Company, Col. William Williams's 4th Regiment, Vermont detached militia (1812)
military_service.notes: Served as Private during the War of 1812 under Capt Robbins, in Col. William Williams's 4th Regiment of Vermont detached militia. Wife Hannah filed widow's pension after his death; Sam's source notes the citation could not be located.

bio.notes: Named for ancestor Thomas Hooker — through maternal grandmother Azubah Norton, granddaughter of Hannah Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Brick Church Cemetery, Greenbush, Sheboygan County WI (per Findagrave).

military_service.notes: DAR Patriot Ancestor A006980; NSSAR Ancestor #110056.
